title: RAG Evaluation with DeepEval (Correctness, Faithfulness, Contextual Relevancy)
one_liner: Score RAG outputs with DeepEval's LLM-as-judge metrics — GEval correctness, faithfulness, and contextual relevancy — over batchable test cases built from questions, ground truths, answers, and retrieved contexts.
problem_solved: Without systematic evaluation you cannot tell whether a RAG pipeline hallucinates, retrieves irrelevant chunks, or answers incorrectly — nor whether a change to chunking, retrieval, or prompting made quality better or worse. This technique turns RAG quality into measurable, thresholded, repeatable test cases scored by a judge LLM.
failure_modes: My RAG system produces answers that aren't supported by the retrieved documents (hallucination / ungrounded generation) and I have no automated way to catch it
I can't tell whether the chunks my retriever returns are actually relevant to the user's question or just vector-similar noise
Generated answers are factually wrong compared to known ground-truth answers and I only find out from user complaints
I changed chunk size / retriever / reranker / prompt and have no regression signal to know if quality improved or degraded
I evaluate my pipeline by eyeballing a few outputs instead of a repeatable pass/fail quality gate
When quality is bad, I can't tell whether the failure is in retrieval (bad context) or generation (bad answer despite good context)
when_to_use: You have (or can curate) a test set of questions with ground-truth answers and want automated correctness scoring
You are A/B comparing pipeline variants (chunking strategy, retriever, reranker, prompt) and need per-metric numbers instead of vibes
You want CI-style regression tests for a RAG system with thresholded pass/fail metrics (e.g. faithfulness >= 0.7)
You need to localize failures: ContextualRelevancyMetric isolates retrieval problems while FaithfulnessMetric and GEval correctness isolate generation problems
You want natural-language reasons attached to failing scores (include_reason=True) to speed up debugging
You already log questions, generated answers, and retrieved contexts and can zip them into test cases at dataset scale
tradeoffs: Every test case x metric pair costs one or more judge-LLM calls to GPT-4-class models — evaluation cost and latency scale linearly with dataset size
The GEval correctness metric requires curated ground-truth answers, which is a real labeling effort for domain corpora
LLM-as-judge scores are nondeterministic and biased by the judge model; thresholds (0.7, 1.0) need calibration per domain and scores can drift when the judge model version changes
Measures answer/context quality only — it does not compute classic retrieval metrics like recall@k, hit rate, or MRR against labeled relevant documents
It is diagnostic, not corrective: it detects hallucination and retrieval noise but you still need other techniques (reranking, context enrichment, query transformation) to fix them
Overkill for quick prototypes; adds the deepeval dependency and an OpenAI API cost footprint
The notebook's examples are toy-scale (e.g. 'what is 3+3?'); production use requires building a genuine test dataset first
how_it_works: The technique operates purely at the evaluation stage, wrapping an existing RAG pipeline's inputs and outputs in DeepEval LLMTestCase objects: each test case carries input (the question), expected_output (ground-truth answer), actual_output (the pipeline's generated answer), and retrieval_context (the list of retrieved chunks). Three judge-LLM metrics are then applied. (1) Correctness via GEval: a custom rubric metric configured with model='gpt-4o', evaluation_params=[EXPECTED_OUTPUT, ACTUAL_OUTPUT], and a plain-English evaluation_steps instruction ('Determine whether the actual output is factually correct based on the expected output'); the judge returns a 0-1 score. (2) FaithfulnessMetric(threshold=0.7, model='gpt-4'): checks whether claims in actual_output are grounded in retrieval_context, catching hallucination independent of ground truth. (3) ContextualRelevancyMetric(threshold=1, model='gpt-4', include_reason=True): checks whether the retrieved chunks are relevant to the input query, with a generated textual reason. Each metric supports metric.measure(test_case) for single-case scoring (exposing .score and .reason), and deepeval's evaluate(test_cases=[...], metrics=[...]) runs a full matrix of cases x metrics in one call. A helper, create_deep_eval_test_cases(questions, gt_answers, generated_answers, retrieved_documents), zips four parallel lists into LLMTestCase objects so an entire evaluation dataset produced by any RAG pipeline can be scored in batch.
key_dependencies: deepeval (evaluate, metrics.GEval, metrics.FaithfulnessMetric, metrics.ContextualRelevancyMetric, test_case.LLMTestCase, test_case.LLMTestCaseParams)
OpenAI API (gpt-4o and gpt-4 used as judge models inside the metrics)
key_code_signals: from deepeval.metrics import GEval, FaithfulnessMetric, ContextualRelevancyMetric
related_techniques: evaluation_grouse: alternative — LLM-judge evaluation using the GroUSE metric framework, plus meta-evaluation of the judge itself
simple_rag: prerequisite — a working RAG pipeline must produce the questions/answers/contexts that feed the test cases
retrieval_with_feedback_loop: composes — evaluation scores like these can drive iterative feedback that improves retrieval over time
choose_chunk_size: composes — use these metrics as the objective when comparing chunking configurations
reranking: measured-by — ContextualRelevancyMetric quantifies whether reranking actually improved retrieved-context quality
reliable_rag: refines — DeepEval formalizes the groundedness/relevancy checks that reliable-RAG pipelines apply ad hoc
summary: DeepEval-based RAG evaluation: wrap each question with its ground-truth answer, generated answer, and retrieved context in an LLMTestCase, then score it with LLM-as-judge metrics. The notebook demonstrates three metrics: GEval correctness (GPT-4o judges the actual output against the expected output using a plain-English rubric), FaithfulnessMetric (checks whether the generated answer is grounded in the retrieval_context — direct hallucination detection with a 0.7 pass threshold), and ContextualRelevancyMetric (checks whether retrieved chunks are actually relevant to the query, with an explanatory reason). A batch evaluate() call runs multiple test cases against multiple metrics at once, and a helper converts four parallel lists (questions, ground truths, generated answers, retrieved documents) into test cases for dataset-scale regression testing. Use it to detect ungrounded/hallucinated answers, retrieval noise, and factual errors, and to get before/after numbers when changing chunking, retrieval, or prompts. Costs: every case-metric pair is a GPT-4-class judge call, correctness needs curated ground-truth answers, and judge scores carry variance and bias that require threshold calibration.
notes_for_chunking: Very small notebook (14 cells, ~1:1 markdown/code ratio) with no stored outputs and no pip-install cell. Structure: cell 0 is a book-promotion markdown block (junk — strip); cell 1 is the consolidated imports cell (deepeval evaluate/metrics/test_case) that every later section depends on — prepend or reference it as context for each chunk; cells 2-7 are three cleanly self-contained metric sections each introduced by a shallow but accurate '### Test X' header (Correctness, faithfulness, contextual relevancy), each containing metric construction + toy LLMTestCase + measure() + print; cells 8-10 form a 'batch evaluate multiple cases with multiple metrics' section whose code depends on objects defined in all three prior sections (not self-contained — chunk with references or merged); cells 11-12 are a self-contained reusable helper (create_deep_eval_test_cases) with its docstring-style markdown header listing the four input lists; cell 13 is an analytics tracker image (junk — strip). Ideal chunking: one chunk per metric section plus one for the batch/helper portion, imports attached to each.
